coredata.dc:title: Restricted-Enhanced Electrochemiluminescence biosensor of low excitation potential carbon quantum dots driven by magnetic Metal-Organic frameworks for sensitive immunoassay of neuron specific enolase
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Jinglong
Li, Min
Cao, Dongmei
Zhang, Jingjing
Wang, Huan
Cao, Wei
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of highly efficient and non-toxic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) plays an important role in immunoassays. However, the high absolute value of the excitation potential and poor controllability make precise detection of low levels difficult. In this work, novel CQDs with low excitation potential were reported to be used for the ultra-sensitive immunoassay of neuron-specific enolase (NSE). It is noteworthy that the narrow bandgap CQDs exhibit a trigger potential of −0.5 V and show excellent applicability of ECL signal at an excitation potential of −0.8 V. This property eliminates side reactions and electrode passivation and significantly improves ECL efficiency. Based on this, the magnetic zeolite-imidazole framework (MZIF) nanoreactor with co-reaction acceleration was employed as a carrier for the in-situ encapsulation of CQDs. The constrained-enhanced ECL sensor platform demonstrated the ability to achieve stable and controllable signal output. A sandwich-type biosensor was constructed by introducing copper sulfide (Cu7S4) as a dual-mechanism quencher, with NSE serving as the target analyte. Moreover, the electrochemical detection method based on the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) properties of Cu7S4, was successfully validated against ECL. The sensors constructed on magnetic electrodes exhibited a bimodal signal output of ECL and DPV, encompassing a wide detection range (0.001 ng/mL − 500 ng/mL) and low detection limit (0.18 pg/mL for ECL and 0.15 pg/mL for DPV). This design offers a novel approach to understanding the efficient emission of carbon dots and provides a solution for the sensitive detection of biomarkers in clinical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Low excitation potential
Restricted-enhanced electrochemiluminescence
Differential pulse voltammetry
Dual signal outputs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of MZIF, CQDs, and MZIF-CQDs
Morphological and structural characterization of Cu7S4 and Cu7S4@Au
Feasibility analysis of the immunosensor for NSE detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A and G) SEM image of MZIF; (B) elemental distribution images of MZIF: (C) C element, (D) O element, (E) Co element, (F) Fe element; (H) HRTEM of CQDs; (I) SEM image of MZIF-CQDs; (J) XPS survey spectra of MZIF; (K) Co scan spectra; (L) Fe Scan spectra.
(A) SEM image of Cu7S4; (B) SEM image of Cu7S4@Au; (C) XRD of Cu7S4; (D) XPS Survey spectra of Cu7S4; (E) S Scan spectra; (F) Cu Scan spectra.

(A) PLE and PL spectra of CQDs dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4, 0.1 M K2S2O8, 0.1 M KCl); (B) ECL spectra of MGCE modified by 6 μL of 2 mg/mL CQDs; (C) ECL spectra of MGCE modified by 6 μL of 2 mg/mL MZIF-CQDs; (D) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, MZIF-CQDs, and PEI-MZIF-CQDs.

(A) ECL current–potential curve and ECL intensity-potential curve of MGCE modified with 6 μL of 2 mg/mL CQDs; (B) ECL intensity-time curve of MGCE modified with 6 μL of 2 mg/mL CQDs; (C) ECL current–potential curve and ECL intensity-potential curve of MGCE modified with 6 μL of 2 mg/mL MZIF-CQDs; (D) ECL intensity-time curve of MGCE modified with 6 μL of 2 mg/mL MZIF-CQDs; (E) Enhanced ECL emission schematic diagram.

(A) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu7S4 and Au NPs and normalized ECL emission spectra of CQDs; (B) ECL quenching performance of Cu7S4 and Cu7S4@Au on MZIF-CQDs (MC); (C) Dual-mode self-correction sensor schematic diagram.

(A) CV, (B) EIS and (C) DPV in different modification states: (a) MGCE, (b) PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE, (c) Ab1/PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE, (d) BSA/Ab1/PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE, (e) NSE/BSA/Ab1/PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE, (f) Cu7S4@Au-Ab2/NSE/BSA/Ab1/PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE.

(A and B) ECL curves and equivalent response. Immunosensors for the detection of different concentrations of NSE: 1 pg/mL, 10 pg/mL, 100 pg/mL, 1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, 50 ng/mL, and 500 ng/mL. (C) Selectivity. (D and E) DPV curves and equivalent response. Immunosensors for the detection of different concentrations of NSE: 1 pg/mL, 10 pg/mL, 100 pg/mL, 1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, 50 ng/mL, and 500 ng/mL. (F) Selectivity. Error bar: ±standard deviation, n = 5.
The construction process of dual-mode immunosensors.

head.ce:title: Restricted-Enhanced Electrochemiluminescence biosensor of low excitation potential carbon quantum dots driven by magnetic Metal-Organic frameworks for sensitive immunoassay of neuron specific enolase

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of highly efficient and non-toxic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) plays an important role in immunoassays. However, the high absolute value of the excitation potential and poor controllability make precise detection of low levels difficult. In this work, novel CQDs with low excitation potential were reported to be used for the ultra-sensitive immunoassay of neuron-specific enolase (NSE). It is noteworthy that the narrow bandgap CQDs exhibit a trigger potential of −0.5 V and show excellent applicability of ECL signal at an excitation potential of −0.8 V. This property eliminates side reactions and electrode passivation and significantly improves ECL efficiency. Based on this, the magnetic zeolite-imidazole framework (MZIF) nanoreactor with co-reaction acceleration was employed as a carrier for the in-situ encapsulation of CQDs. The constrained-enhanced ECL sensor platform demonstrated the ability to achieve stable and controllable signal output. A sandwich-type biosensor was constructed by introducing copper sulfide (Cu7S4) as a dual-mechanism quencher, with NSE serving as the target analyte. Moreover, the electrochemical detection method based on the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) properties of Cu7S4, was successfully validated against ECL. The sensors constructed on magnetic electrodes exhibited a bimodal signal output of ECL and DPV, encompassing a wide detection range (0.001 ng/mL − 500 ng/mL) and low detection limit (0.18 pg/mL for ECL and 0.15 pg/mL for DPV). This design offers a novel approach to understanding the efficient emission of carbon dots and provides a solution for the sensitive detection of biomarkers in clinical research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a luminescence process in which energy is radiatively released from an excited state luminophore, which is produced by a ground-state luminophore that is first electrically stimulated and then chemically converted [1,2]. Due to the advantages of flexible voltage manipulation [3], low background signal [4], high sensitivity [5], and simplicity of equipment [6], ECL has gained considerable popularity in fundamental applications such as food analysis [7], bio-imaging [8], metal ion detection [9], and disease biomarkers analysis [10]. However, the variety of detection targets places significant demands on the selection of ECL luminophores. The luminophores must not only exhibit high intrinsic luminescence efficiency but also demonstrate stability and control during the detection process. Given these considerations, the diversification of conventional luminophores and the multidirectional development of novel luminophores are receiving considerable attention.

Carbon dots, a collective term for carbon-based nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm, have superior properties compared to traditional ECL luminophores, especially with regard to low biotoxicity. Based on their shape, crystallinity, and quantum confinement effects, they can be categorized into graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [11]. These materials possess several advantageous properties, including excellent electrical conductivity, tunable size, multi-structured surface states, and an abundance of functional groups. These properties enable a wide range of operations in ECL, especially in terms of lowering the excitation potential and increasing efficiency. For example, Yuan et al. developed a carbon dots-based anodic ECL with low excitation potential and with hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) as a co-reactant [12]. However, it is important to note that H₂O₂ tends to interfere with the ECL signal due to decomposition. If the concentration of H₂O₂ is not precisely regulated, the activity of the bioanalytical targets may be impaired by elevated concentrations of active intermediates. The development of cathodic ECL with the less irritating potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) as a co-reactant offers a solution to overcome this deficiency [13]. Nevertheless, the emergence of higher excitation potentials and the passivation of electrodes is a potential source of inaccuracy and uncontrollability. It is also important to note that the dissolved oxygen-based K2S2O8-O2 system exhibits a significant ECL signal when the absolute value of the excitation potential at the cathode is greater than 1.4. This further affects the analytical accuracy. In addition, the complete elimination of O2 is a challenge that can only be achieved by using specialized equipment. Consequently, the development of ECL luminophores based on carbon dots with a low excitation potential at the cathode can address a variety of challenges simultaneously, and pave the way for the exploration of general ECL luminescence devices.

A significant disadvantage of using small-sized luminophores is the occurrence of leakage during the detection procedure. This necessitates the use of highly effective carriers for encapsulation or embedding, with carriers that enhance luminescence proving to be the optimal solution. Zeolite-imidazole framework 67 (ZIF-67), a representative nanocage-shaped metal–organic framework (MOF), possesses properties such as large pore size and ease of synthesis, which make it an excellent encapsulated carrier [14]. The restricted reaction after in-situ encapsulation can increase the probability of collisions between the luminophores and the co-reactant intermediates, shorten the path of chemical reactions, reduce the loss of non-radiative energy, and ultimately enhance the emission of ECL signals [15]. However, the immobilization of the luminescent substrate on the electrode surface usually requires the incorporation of film formers (e.g., chitosan and Nafion) to increase emission stability. This can hinder electron transfer and reduce ECL efficiency [16]. Given the availability of commercially available magnetic electrodes, magnetic modification of the carrier could be a viable way to further improve the ECL signal. Fortunately, the incorporation of iron into ZIF-67 leads to the emergence of ferromagnetism. Magnetic adsorption between the magnetic electrode and the ferromagnetic ZIF in which the luminophore is encapsulated can effectively bind the luminophore to the electrode interface, and thus prevent the occurrence of signal degradation due to leakage of the luminophore.

In addition to the advantages offered by luminophores, the choice of specific detection strategies can further increase the sensitivity of ECL immunoassays. Signal-off sensing strategies can significantly increase the response intensity in the region of weak fluctuations. However, the mechanism requires superior material to quench the light signal. Copper sulfide (Cu7S4) which has excellent electron modulation and light absorption properties, is widely used in biomedical applications. For instance, Sun et al. employed it in combination with silicon dioxide to synergistically treat malignant tumors in a photochemical kinetic and photothermal manner [17], while Li et al. introduced it into solar-driven interfacial evaporation [18]. The numerous applications show that Cu7S4 is an excellent light-absorbing material that can effectively quench signals. In addition, the conduction band of CQDs is in close proximity to the valence band of Cu7S4, which facilitates the transition of high-energy electrons from the former to the latter with minimal resistance. This can reduce the radiative jumps of the high-energy electrons and facilitate further annihilation of the ECL signals by electron transfer. The dual-quenching mechanism is an effective method for signal quenching and ultrasensitive detection of disease markers. It is encouraging to note that Cu7S4 has proven to be an excellent electrochemical sensing material. Its excellent properties in differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) offer the possibility of constructing a sensor with two outputs that can be easily intercalibrated to improve accuracy.

In this study, CQDs with low excitation potential were synthesized to eliminate multiple negative effects associated with high potential and applied toward practice by performing ECL immunoassays against neuron-specific enolase. First, solvothermal synthesized CQDs with abundant functional groups showed strong ECL intensities at low excitation potential. Subsequently, the CQDs were in-situ encapsulated in magnetic ZIF (MZIF) to construct a magnetically adsorbed sensor platform that exhibited limited-enhanced ECL activity. Moreover, this sensor platform exhibited more stable and effective ECL stability than pure CQDs. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), an amino-rich water-soluble polymer, was modified onto the composite surface for the purpose of immobilizing capture antibodies, thereby serving as an efficient sensing platform. Furthermore, Cu7S4 was employed as a signal quenching agent, and the creation of a sandwich-type “signal-off” sensing strategy was achieved by successfully realizing the dual quenching of ECL through light absorption and high-energy electron transfer. The “signal-on” electrochemical model was further established with the DPV signal of Cu7S4, and the detection accuracy was improved by mutual correction. The detection range of this dual-mode sensor wide detection range with an excellent detection limit. It is believed that the synthesized luminophores can be excellent candidates for the development of new ECL systems and also provide good support for the analysis of sensitive disease markers.

The CQDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method based on previous reports [19]. In a typical procedure, 4.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in 20.0 mL of formic acid (FA), and simultaneously 8.0 g of urea was dissolved in another 20.0 mL of FA. The two solutions were mixed and transferred to a high-pressure reaction vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. The reaction was carried out at a constant temperature of 160 °C for 4 h in a thermostatic reactor. After the reaction, the resulting product was thoroughly mixed with 80.0 mL of ethanol and centrifuged (10000 rpm, 5 min). The supernatant was discarded, and the precipitate was washed at least three times with ethanol. Finally, the product was dried in a vacuum desiccator at −40 °C for 24 h to obtain CQDs.

Initially, 1.2 g of FeSO4·7H2O and 10.0 mg of the previously prepared CQDs were dissolved in 10.0 mL of water, denoted as solution A. In a separate solution, 1.2 g of cobalt acetate tetrahydrate was dissolved in 10.0 mL of water, denoted as solution B. Subsequently, solutions A and B were then mixed and added to a 10.0 mL solution of 2-methylimidazole (2-MI) with stirring. The resulting mixture was stirred for 30 min and then allowed to stand for 20 h, resulting in a deep blue solution. The target material was separated from impurities by magnetic adsorption, followed by three washes with deionized water to obtain pure MZIF-CQDs. The preparation of MZIF followed the same procedure as described above for MZIF-CQDs, except for the introduction of CQDs. A mixture of 5.0 mL PEI (1.0 %) and 10 mL MZIF-CQDs dispersion (1.0 mg/mL) was stirred overnight at room temperature. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min and washing three times with deionized water, the resulting PEI-MZIF-CQDs were vacuum-dried at 60 °C and stored at 4 °C.

Cu7S4 synthesis was carried out according to the method proposed by Cao et al., using a simple hydrothermal approach [20]. In a typical procedure, 66.7 mg of PVP was dissolved in 33.0 mL of CuSO4 solution (0.01 M) and mixed well using ultrasonication. Then, 8.3 mL of NaOH solution (1.5 M) was added to the above solution with constant stirring. After one minute, 8.3 mL of ascorbic acid (0.1 M) was added rapidly, resulting in a color change from blue to green and then yellow. 15 min later, the solution turned orange-red. Subsequently, 1.7 mL of thiourea (0.2 M) was added dropwise to the above solution, and stirring was continued for 2 min before the solution was placed in a 100.0 mL Teflon lined reactor, and heated at 90 °C for 6 h. The obtained product was cooled to room temperature, washed more than three times by centrifugation at 8000 rpm, and dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The resulting dark gray product was ground and stored for further use. To prepare Cu7S4@Au, 20.0 mg of Cu7S4 was sealed with 20.0 mL of pre-prepared gold nanoparticles in a 40.0 mL centrifuge tube. The sealed tube was wrapped in aluminum foil and placed on an oscillator for shaking overnight. After centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) and washing with deionized water, Cu7S4@Au was obtained. Cu7S4@Au-Ab2 was obtained by incubating 5.0 mg of Cu7S4@Au with 1 mL of Ab2 (2.0 μg/mL) at room temperature for 12 h, followed by low-temperature centrifugation (4 °C, 8000 rpm, 10 min).

The fabrication process of the immunosensor was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Before use, magnetic glassy carbon electrodes (MGCE, Φ = 4 mm) were polished with different sizes of Al2O3 slurry on chamois leather until the potential difference measured in potassium ferricyanide solution was less than 110 mV. Then, 6.0 μL of PEI-MZIF-CQDs suspension (2.5 mg/mL) was trimmed on the cleaned GCE surface and dried at room temperature to form PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE. Subsequently, 6.0 μL of Ab1 (pH 6.8, 1.0 μg/mL) activated by a cross-linking agent was dropped onto the incompletely dried PEI-MZIF-CQDs/MGCE surface, and after 2 h, 2.0 μL of BSA (0.1 %) was added to block nonspecific active sites. After the electrode surface was free of visible water beads, the sensor was incubated with different concentrations of antigen in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Finally, unbound antigen was rinsed away, and 6.0 μL of Cu7S4@Au-Ab2 suspension (pH 6.8, 2.5 mg/mL) was added to complete the sensor construction.

The ECL measurement parameters included a potential range of −0.8 − 0 V, a scan rate of 0.2 V/s, and a photomultiplier tube voltage of 800 V. The electrochemical measurement parameters for CV experiments during the sensor construction process were an onset potential of −0.2 V, a high potential of 0.6 V, and a scan rate of 50 mV/s. EIS experiments included an amplitude of 7 mV and a frequency range of 0.1 − 1 × 105 Hz. The DPV measurement parameters included an initial potential of −0.2 V, a high potential of 0.6 V, a pulse amplitude of 50 mV, and a pulse period of 1 s. All measurements were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of MZIF, CQDs, and MZIF-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Various analytical techniques were used to investigate the morphological features and structural properties of the luminescent matrix, including field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). SEM analysis depicted in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1G revealed the morphological characteristics of MZIF. In contrast to the conventional polyhedral structure of ZIF-67, MZIF exhibited a microflower-like structure composed of surface-stacked nanosheets. This structure may arise from the magnetization-driven growth of metal and ligands on the polyhedral core during the static process. Element distribution images (Fig. 1B-1F) confirmed the successful incorporation of Fe elements into ZIF-67. Based on Energy-Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) (Figure S1), the atomic ratio of Fe to Co was close to 1, which might be the result of the equivalent substitution of Co by Fe during the coordination process. XRD analysis (Figure S2) confirmed the presence of characteristic peaks of the magnetic spinel oxides Fe3O4 (JCPDS: 99–0073) and CoFe2O4 (JCPDS: 79–1744) in MZIF, indicating a possible magnetic origin of MZIF. In addition, the small field characteristic peaks of MZIF overlapped with those of ZIF-67, providing evidence that the synthesis of MZIF was successful. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1H shows that the prepared carbon dots are CQDs with distinct lattice fringes, having an interplanar spacing of 0.19 nm. MZIF-CQDs (Fig. 1I) showed more pronounced adhesion-induced aggregation compared to MZIF, possibly due to the numerous organic groups on the surface of the CQDs. The HRTEM (Figure S3) images successfully confirmed the encapsulation of CQDs within the MZIF. To gain insights into the magnetic behavior of MZIF and MZIF-CQDs, hysteresis loop tests and macroscopic magneto-absorption tests were performed. As illustrated in Figure S4A, MZIF displayed ferromagnetism at room temperature with a forced magnetic field of 0.013 T. The saturation magnetization strength was 23.59 Am2/kg, while the residual magnetization strength was 2.52 Am2/kg at room temperature. The observed decay of the hysteresis loop intensity in MZIF-CQDs (Figure S4B) provided evidence that the doping of two opposite magnetic materials has occurred. This indicated that the introduction of CQDs weakened the magnetic properties of MZIF, and provided further evidence of the successful encapsulation of CQDs in the material. In addition, the magneto-absorption method was employed to confirm the magnetic properties of MZIF and MZIF-CQDs. The results (Figure S5) demonstrated that both materials could be rapidly adsorbed on the magnet surface within a short period. To further evaluate the structure of MZIF, its XPS spectrum (Fig. 1J) was analyzed. The characteristic peaks of Co 2p and Fe 2p confirm the presence of these two elements in MZIF. The deconvoluted XPS spectrum of Co 2p (Fig. 1K) contains several components, with the blue and green emission contributions detected in Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively, being due to Co-O and Co-N coordination. In addition, the Fe 2p spectra exhibit complex multistate splitting and satellite features due to the high spin of the complex. In Fig. 1L, binding energy peaks of Fe2+ at 710 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 724 eV (Fe 2p1/2) are observed, while the peaks at 712 eV (Fe 2p3/2) and 726 eV (Fe 2p1/2) correspond to Fe3+. This suggested that MZIF possesses the mixed valence metal ions. In addition to the limited-enhanced ECL emission, MZIF with multivalent properties can function as a co-reaction accelerator to improve the luminescence efficiency by catalyzing the reduction of K2S2O8 to enhance the chemical reaction of radicals [21,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of Cu7S4 and Cu7S4@Au
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental techniques such as SEM, XRD, and XPS were employed to verify the successful synthesis of Cu7S4. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, Cu7S4 exhibited 18 facets, including 6 regular square faces and 12 hexagonal faces. The average size was approximately 400 nm, as shown by particle size distribution calculations (Figure S6). The regular and homogeneous morphology of Cu7S4 facilitates effective binding with gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). The Au NPs were uniformly distributed in the range of 20 − 40 nm (Figure S7A and S7B) and adhered firmly and uniformly to the surface of Cu7S4 (Fig. 2B). The EDX spectra in Figure S7C and Figure S7D demonstrated the uniform presence of Cu, S, and Au in the Cu7S4@Au. The Cu/S atomic ratio (Figure S7E) was measured to be 1.81, which is very similar to the theoretical stoichiometric ratio of Cu7S4. The XRD patterns of Cu7S4 are depicted in Fig. 2C. They show peaks at 26.53°, 29.57°, 31.20°, 34.02°, 36.45°, 42.39°, 46.65°, and 48.76°, which correspond to the typical diffraction patterns of the monoclinic Cu7S4 (JCPDS No.23−0598) crystal lattice planes, namely (16 0 0), (804), (18 2 1), (20 0 1), (155), (6 14 1), (0 16 0), and (886). To gain further insight into the chemical state of Cu7S4, XPS measurements were conducted. As can be seen in Fig. 2D, the XPS spectrum shows clear sulfur and copper peaks. In Fig. 2E, the peaks at 161.78 eV and 162.98 eV were attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, while a lower-intensity peak at 168.78 eV was assigned to surface-oxidized sulfate ions (SO4 2−). The presence of sulfate ions could be attributed to the interaction between oxygen and sulfur ions. Fig. 2F illustrates the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p. The peaks at 932.28 eV and 952.18 eV were attributed to the low-intensity signals of Cu1+ 2p3/2 and Cu1+ 2p1/2, respectively, while the peaks at 934.18 eV and 954.48 eV corresponded to the higher-intensity signals of Cu2+ 2p3/2 and Cu2+ 2p1/2. The results indicate that the material possesses both Cu1+ and Cu2+ atoms simultaneously, which is essential for its application as an ECL quencher and DPV indicator.

The optical characteristics of CQDs were investigated through the analysis of their photoluminescence, ECL, and Fourier Transform Infrared Absorption (FT-IR) spectra. The fluorescence excitation wavelength (Fig. 3 A) was observed at 429 nm, with an emission wavelength of 528 nm. In a K2S2O8 solution, excited at a potential of −0.8 V, the ECL emission (Fig. 3B) covered a wavelength range of 450 nm to 800 nm, with a central wavelength of 570 nm. The redshift of the emission spectrum is referred to as the recognized surface state ECL emission of quantum dots. In addition, MZIF CQDs have the same emission characteristics (Fig. 3C) as pure CQDs. FT-IR (Fig. 3D) revealed characteristic peaks at 3433 cm−1 for O-H stretching vibrations, 3159 cm−1, and 1627 cm−1 for N-H stretching and bending vibrations, respectively. The characteristic peak at 1710 cm−1 indicated stretching vibrations of C=O in carboxyl or intramolecular amide, while the peak at 1570 cm−1 was attributed to O-C=O stretching vibrations in carboxyl groups. These results suggested the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide groups in the synthesized CQDs, enhancing the conjugation on the CQD's surface. The large size and abundant surface groups reduced the energy gap between the conduction and valence bands, lowering the excitation potential and electron transition energy [23–25]. Upon the formation of MZIF-CQDs, a notable reduction in surface groups can be observed. Nevertheless, the weak carboxyl peak persisted, which could be attributed to the extension of carbon dot surface functional groups in the nano-channels that were not involved in coordination. Subsequently, polyethyleneimine (PEI) was polymerized onto the surface of MZIF-CQDs for amination, which in turn enabled the incubation of the capture antibody (Ab1). The results of infrared spectroscopy indicated the presence of a significant number of amino groups on the surface, which is essential for the binding of the Ab1, and thus the construction of a highly loaded sensing interface.

Firstly, ECL measurements of CQDs were obtained by cyclic voltammetry (CV) excitation. As shown in Fig. 4 A and Figure S8, the ECL trigger potential of CQDs at the cathode is −0.5 V, with the highest value existing at −0.8 V. This feature is far superior to the relevant studies published so far. Furthermore, the emission of ECL is observed to be independent of the test environment (Figure S9) and test platform (Figure S10). It is well known that the excitation potential of luminophores is related to their bandgap energy and band positions. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) curves, Tauc plots, and valence-band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) data (Figure S11) were statistically and carried out for the illustration of the low excitation potential ECL of the CQDs. The computed bandgap energy of the CQDs was 1.66 eV, and the valence-band position was 1.52 eV, resulting in a conduction band position of −0.14 eV. Compared to other cathodic carbon dots, the synthesized CQDs exhibit a reduced band gap, resulting in lower energy required for excitation and a positive shift in the ECL excitation potential [23,26]. Within the window of reduced potential, the low excitation potential ECL process can spare the high excitation potential from biologically active molecules, eliminate the interference of the dissolved oxygen system, prevent electrode passivation, and effectively achieve highly sensitive and precise analysis of biomolecules. In the ECL process (equations 1–4), at an excitation potential of −0.8 V, electrons were injected into the surface of CQDs to produce CQDs•-, while peroxydisulfate (S2O8 2-) underwent a reduction reaction to obtain strong oxidizing sulfate radicals (SO4 •-). Subsequently, CQDs•- react chemically with SO4 •- to produce excited state CQDs (CQDs*). Ultimately, CQDs* realize light emission during radiative leaps back to the ground state. However, CQDs drop-cast onto the magnetic electrodes detached from the surface due to higher water dispersion, resulting in extremely poor signal stability, as shown in Fig. 4B. The in-situ encapsulation of the CQDs by MZIF effectively addresses this issue. As illustrated in Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D, the MZIF-CQDs exhibit markedly enhanced reduction currents and ECL signals, accompanied by enhanced signal stability, rendering them suitable for analytical detection. As proposed (Fig. 4E), the presence of MIZF enables signals of CQDs by multiple means. In detail, MZIF achieves in-situ confinement of CQDs, and the presence of mixed valence states enables more intermediate radicals to be obtained through the co-reactant acceleration effect. The presence of nanopores promotes the proximity confinement reaction between the luminophore and co-reactant intermediate radicals, which in turn leads to signal amplification. Furthermore, the magnetic adsorption interaction between the ferromagnetic reactor and MGCE contributes to the stabilization of the reaction of CQDs. It is of great significance that a comprehensive range of ECL efficiencies were tested and calculated (Table S1). The results demonstrated that the MZIF-CQDs exhibited the highest ECL efficiency value of 2.8 % at −0.8 V. It can be reasonably concluded that MZIF-CQDs represent a comprehensive and enhanced luminescence platform.

ECL mechanism:
(1)
CQDs +
e -
→ C Q D
s
∙ -
- 0.8 V
(2)
S 2
O 8
2 -
+
e -
→ S
O 4
2 -
+ S
O 4
∙ -
(3)
CQD
s
∙ -
+ S
O 4
∙ -
→ C Q D s ∗ + S
O 4
2 -
(4)
CQDs ∗ → C Q D s + h v

The favorable characteristics of the luminescent substrate have facilitated the development of “signal-off” ECL sensing strategies. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the normalized spectra demonstrate a significant degree of spectral overlap between the 450 nm − 800 nm ECL emission region of CQDs and the UV–vis absorption regions of Cu7S4 and Au NPs. This provides a robust theoretical foundation for Cu7S4@Au as a light quencher (equation (5)). Subsequently, UV–vis DRS, Tauc plots, and VB-XPS (Figure S12) of Cu7S4 were subjected to further analysis. The band gap and valence band positions of Cu7S4 were demonstrated to be 1.67 eV and 0.05 eV, respectively, resulting in a conduction band position of −1.72 eV. The proximity of its valence band position to the conduction band position of the CQDs renders it an ECL electron-transfer acceptor (equations 6–7) [27,28]. In terms of ECL performance, both Cu7S4 and Cu7S4@Au demonstrate robust ECL quenching capabilities (Fig. 5B). At the concentrations tested, the ECL quenching rates were 94 % and 90 %, respectively. The good UV–vis absorption of Au NPs resulted in strong light absorption for ECL, but the improved electrical conductivity (Figure S13) decreased the composite Cu7S4@Au quenching ability. Nevertheless, the Cu7S4@Au can be considered an excellent signal label, due to its high binding capacity to the detection antibody. In addition, Cu7S4 has a differential pulse voltammetry signal (Figure S14) between 0 V and 0.2 V due to the oxidation of Cu1+ to Cu2+ (equations (8)) and is not affected by the substrate (Figure S15), allowing it to function as an electrochemical “signal-on” indicator for self-correction in dual-mode detection (Fig. 5C).

→ C Q D s + C

S 4
Principle of DPV Sensor:
(8)
C
u
1 +
- e → C
u
2 +

The modification process of the biosensor was characterized through CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the modification of the electrode surface with PEI-MZIF-CQDs (curve b) resulted in a current that was lower than that of the bare electrode (curve a) due to the low electrical conductivity of the MOF. With the help of EDC and NHS, the Ab1 bound to the surface of PEI-MZIF-CQDs. After BSA blocked the non-specific active site, NSE was recognized by specific incubation. Due to the weak conductivity of proteins, the current gradually decreased (curves c, d, e). After specific recognition of the sensor by the signal probe Cu7S4@Au-Ab2, the resistivity of the sensor reached a maximum and the current becomes a minimum for the duration of the process (curves f). Fig. 6B illustrates the electron transfer resistance (Ret) on the electrodes at different binding stages. The observation that the resistance consistently increases was consistent with the conclusion that the current of the CV consistently decreases, indicating that the construction of the sensing platform is successful. Furthermore, a DPV signal test was conducted during the construction process (Fig. 6C), which revealed that a substantial DPV signal was observed exclusively when Cu7S4@Au-Ab2 was present. This finding substantiated the viability of developing an electrochemical sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility analysis of the immunosensor for NSE detection
ce:section.ce:para: This study presents a comprehensive analysis of the feasibility of an immunosensor optimized for the detection of NSE. Under optimized experimental conditions (Figure S16 and Figure S17), the immunosensor demonstrated a robust linear response within a concentration range of 0.001 to 500 ng/mL (Fig. 7 ). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.18 pg/mL and 0.15 pg/mL, respectively, employing ECL and DPV methods. The assessment of selectivity (Fig. 7C and Fig. 7F), a crucial parameter in evaluating sensor performance, was conducted. The biosensor exhibited negligible interference from CEA, AFB1, AFP, and CYFRA21-1 at 10 ng/mL. Both the ECL and DPV signals exhibited notable variations in response to the target and mixed samples, thereby establishing the excellent selectivity of the biosensor. Moreover, Figure S18 demonstrates that the signal stability is within an acceptable range. Furthermore, the results of the batch-by-batch experiments (Figure S19) demonstrated a variation of less than 5.0 %, indicating that the biosensor exhibits exceptional reproducibility. The efficacy of the immunosensor was additionally corroborated through the measurement of NSE concentrations in serum samples (Table S2). The recoveries from serum samples at varying concentrations exhibited a range of 94.8 % to 103.8 %. These findings demonstrate the potential of the proposed method for trace analysis of NSE in serum, indicating its applicability for practical sample detection and suggesting significant clinical utility.

This study introduces, for the first time, carbon dots excited at −0.8 V into an ECL system and proposes a biosensor with dual signal output employing ECL and DPV. The feasibility of this immunosensor is validated using NSE as the analytical model. The signal strength and stabilization of carbon dots were achieved through the encapsulation of a magnetic nanoreactor, coupled with a magnetic electrode. Cu7S4@Au was employed as both an ECL quencher and DPV signal indicator, facilitating the dual-signal detection of the target analyte. The integration of two independent signals provided mutual verification, thereby enhancing the credibility of the analytical results. The utilization of low excited potential carbon dots and the incorporation of dual-signal output contribute to the advancement of immunosensor technology for improved accuracy and precision in the detection of biomarkers like NSE.

Jinglong Fang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Min Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Dongmei Cao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jingjing Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Huan Wang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wei Cao: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Qin Wei: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.
